question_text: Let ℱ= {𝑓: [𝑎, 𝑏] →ℝ | 𝑓 is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (𝑎, 𝑏)}. Which one of the following options is correct?

options.A: There exists a non-constant 𝑓∈ℱ such that |𝑓(𝑥) −𝑓(𝑦)| ≤|𝑥−𝑦|2 for all 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈[𝑎, 𝑏]
options.B: If 𝑓∈ℱ and 𝑥0 ∈(𝑎, 𝑏), then there exist distinct 𝑥1, 𝑥2 ∈[𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓(𝑥1)−𝑓(𝑥2) 𝑥1−𝑥2 = 𝑓′(𝑥0)
options.C: Let 𝑓∈ℱ and 𝑓′(𝑥) ≥0 for all 𝑥∈(𝑎, 𝑏). If 𝑓′ is zero only at two distinct points, then 𝑓 is strictly increasing
options.D: Let 𝑓∈ℱ. If 𝑓′(𝑥1) < 𝑐< 𝑓′(𝑥2) for some 𝑥1, 𝑥2 ∈(𝑎, 𝑏), then there may NOT exist an 𝑥0 ∈(𝑥1, 𝑥2) such that 𝑓′(𝑥0) = 𝑐
